title: Privileged User Activity Monitoring

compliance.assurance_rationale: Monitors privileged and identity-management actions in Catalyst Center to support least-privilege review and incident detection.
compliance.controlObjective: Surfaces who exercises powerful accounts and identity controls on the management system, helping enforce NIST 800-53 AC-6 (least privilege) in practice through detective review.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Dashboard or alert `UC-5.13.47` with monthly privileged-activity printout and reviewer sign-off for access governance.

compliance.assurance_rationale: Retention of auditable events for high-risk administrative actions on Catalyst Center for security monitoring.
compliance.controlObjective: Confirms the auditable event stream includes privileged-identity and role-management work needed for log review (AU-2) on the control plane.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Same saved search, tied to the SIEM or log-management retention schedule and sampling notes for the audit function.

dataSources: index=catalyst, sourcetype cisco:dnac:audit:logs (Catalyst Center audit; user, role, permission, and admin-sounding auditRequestType values)
spl: index=catalyst sourcetype="cisco:dnac:audit:logs" (auditUserName="admin" OR auditRequestType="*USER*" OR auditRequestType="*ROLE*" OR auditRequestType="*PERMISSION*") | stats count as action_count dc(auditRequestType) as unique_actions values(auditRequestType) as action_types by auditUserName, auditIpAddress | sort -action_count
description: Monitors activity from privileged accounts (admins, super-admins) and user/role management actions to detect potential abuse or compromised credentials.
value: Privileged accounts can cause maximum damage. Monitoring their activity detects unauthorized use, credential compromise, and insider threats.
implementation: Enable the `audit_logs` input in the Cisco Catalyst TA pointing to `index=catalyst`. The TA polls audit log data from the Catalyst Center Intent API `/dna/intent/api/v1/audit/logs` every 5 minutes. Key fields: `auditRequestType`, `auditDescription`, `auditUserName`, `auditTimestamp`, `auditIpAddress`.
visualization: Table (auditUserName, auditIpAddress, action_count, unique_actions, action_types), alert on spikes or new admin sources.
knownFalsePositives: Planned break-glass, access reviews, or IdP role pushes can spike USER, ROLE, and PERMISSION audit types with no security incident. A service account for automation and a shared jump host IP can sit at the top of the table for approved work. The literal `auditUserName="admin"` may never exist in your tenant; replace with your true superuser naming pattern.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
‚Ä¢ Cisco Catalyst Add-on (7538) with **audit_logs** writing `cisco:dnac:audit:logs` to `index=catalyst` (Intent `GET` audit log API; typical 300s poll).
‚Ä¢ Tuning is mandatory: wildcards such as `*USER*` can over-match; add a lookup of allowed `auditRequestType` values in production, or list explicit request types for SOX/PCI evidence.
‚Ä¢ `docs/implementation-guide.md` and `docs/guides/catalyst-center.md`.

Step 1 ‚Äî Configure data collection
‚Ä¢ Baseline your jump-box and VPN `auditIpAddress` values before any spike alert; a hot IP can be ‚Äúnormal‚Äù for 24/7 NOC work.

Step 2 ‚Äî Privileged-activity view
```spl
index=catalyst sourcetype="cisco:dnac:audit:logs" (auditUserName="admin" OR auditRequestType="*USER*" OR auditRequestType="*ROLE*" OR auditRequestType="*PERMISSION*") | stats count as action_count dc(auditRequestType) as unique_actions values(auditRequestType) as action_types by auditUserName, auditIpAddress | sort -action_count
```

Understanding this SPL (identity and RBAC on Catalyst)
**Privileged user monitoring** ‚Äî Ranks the loudest `auditUserName`+`auditIpAddress` pairs for identity-related `auditRequestType` strings, with a separate branch for a literal `admin` username. Replace the literal and wildcards to match your tenant and naming standard.

**Pipeline walkthrough**
‚Ä¢ Filter to likely high-privilege work ‚Üí `stats` for volume, `dc` of type, and `values` of type strings ‚Üí `sort` by `action_count`.

Step 3 ‚Äî Validate
‚Ä¢ Cross-check the top 10 with your IdP: confirm whether each `auditUserName` is expected in that window (break-glass, TAC, or service principal). HR-driven bulk user sync can legitimately run up counts.

Step 4 ‚Äî Operationalize
‚Ä¢ Optional alert: a **new** `auditIpAddress` for a **known** admin account, implemented with a `lookup` of historical pairs; throttle and route to the identity team, not a generic on-call.

Step 5 ‚Äî Troubleshooting
‚Ä¢ After a Catalyst or TA upgrade, re-run `| stats count by auditRequestType` to refresh the wildcard list. No rows: filter too tight or the audit user never uses those strings. Time skew: align `auditTimestamp` with Splunk `_time` for PAM cross-checks‚Äîfix NTP on Catalyst, not in SPL alone, for evidential use.
grandmaExplanation: We keep a close eye on what your most powerful admin accounts are doing on Catalyst Center, catching anything unusual that could mean trouble.
